nativeRange.habitat: Shallow tropical reef walls, overhangs, and lower reef slopes with reduced light and gentle flow; commonly found plating outward from shaded ledges

decorPreferences: stable rockwork base for plating growth
ample open space around the colony (15+ cm clearance from neighbors)

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae. Target feeding once or twice weekly with small meaty foods such as mysis shrimp, baby brine shrimp, Reef Roids, or CYCLOP-EEZE is generally beneficial for growth and coloration. Feeding tends to occur at night when sweeper tentacles are extended; a turkey baster or pipette can be used for targeted delivery.

goodWith: non-aggressive LPS corals placed well out of reach
avoidWith: corals within sweeper tentacle range (15+ cm)
aggressive LPS such as Galaxea or Hammer placed nearby

summary: Echinophyllia is an LPS genus sold collectively as 'Chalice Coral,' prized for vivid color morphs. Generally intermediate care; prefers moderate light, gentle flow, and ample spacing from neighbors due to aggressive sweeper tentacles.
careNotes:
Trade name note: 'Chalice Coral' is a broad commercial umbrella name applied to several genera in the aquarium hobby — primarily Echinophyllia (family Lobophylliidae) and Oxypora (see [oxypora-coral], also Lobophylliidae — the true chalice clade), as well as Mycedium (see [mycedium-coral], family Merulinidae per WoRMS / Budd et al. 2012 — convergent plating morphology, not close phylogeny), Echinopora (Merulinidae), Pectinia, and Lithophyllon. All share similar plating or encrusting foliaceous growth forms and broadly similar husbandry, but differ in polyp texture, eye pattern, and lighting sweet spots. Echinophyllia proper are reclassified from the former family Pectiniidae into Lobophylliidae as of the 2012 molecular revision (confirmed by WoRMS). The genus Oxyphyllia (Yabe & Eguchi, 1935) is a recognized synonym.

Aggression: Echinophyllia are considered among the more aggressive LPS corals in the reef hobby. They extend long sweeper tentacles — commonly reported at 15 cm (6 inches) or more at night, with some accounts noting 15–20 cm on healthy colonies — and also secrete toxic mucus that can damage neighboring tissue on contact. Placement must account for full nighttime extension; a minimum of 15 cm clear space in every direction is advisable, and spacing of 20+ cm from other LPS is preferred.

Lighting: Chalice corals generally thrive at moderate light levels. Sources converge on 75–200 PAR at coral depth as a workable range, with 100–150 PAR being a common sweet spot. Oxypora species tend to prefer the lower end (50–100 PAR), while Mycedium can handle up to 150–200 PAR. New specimens should be acclimated gradually over 2–3 weeks starting at lower light.

Growth forms: Depending on placement and species, Echinophyllia may grow as flat plates, encrusting sheets, or shallow cup/bowl shapes. Thin-skeleton morphs (e.g., Miami Hurricane) can plate rapidly; thick-skeleton morphs add approximately 6–7 mm per month. In the wild, colonies can exceed 60 cm in diameter; captive specimens typically reach 15–30 cm before tank constraints limit further expansion.

Water quality: Clean, stable water chemistry is important. Nitrate around 2–10 ppm and phosphate 0.03–0.1 ppm are generally reported as workable; extremely low-nutrient systems may cause color loss. Calcium, alkalinity, and magnesium supplementation as for other LPS corals is recommended.

Fragging: Relatively straightforward — colonies can be fragged with bone cutters or a tile saw. Frags of 2.5 cm or larger encrust and recover well. Dipping new frags in a coral dip before introduction is advisable to prevent hitchhiker pests.

Temperature note: Sources ranged from 72°F–84°F across references. The conservative lower bound (LiveAquaria/AquariumSource: 72°F/22°C) may reflect shipping tolerance rather than ideal husbandry; most specialist guides (ExtremeCoral, TopShelf) converge on 76–78°F (24–25.5°C) as the preferred range. The filed range of 23–27°C represents the averaged cross-source consensus.
breedingNotes: Fragging is the primary propagation method in captivity. Frags are typically cut 2.5 cm or larger and mounted on frag plugs or rubble with coral glue; they encrust and begin growing within a few weeks under stable conditions. Sexual broadcast spawning occurs on wild reefs but is not practiced in home aquaria.

additional.name: Extreme Corals – Care Requirements of Echinophyllia in Home Reef Aquariums
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (76–80°F), pH (8.1–8.4), alkalinity (8–10 dKH), salinity (1.024–1.026), calcium (400–450 ppm), magnesium (1250–1350 ppm), PAR (80–150), and feeding (1–2×/week). Minor temperature disagreement with LiveAquaria resolved by averaging across sources.

additional.name: MetaReef – Chalice Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Confirmed care level (intermediate), flow (low to moderate, 20–40× turnover), PAR (50–150, with genus-level variation), and aggression. Provided multi-genera taxonomy context (Echinophyllia, Oxypora, Mycedium in Lobophylliidae/Merulinidae).

additional.name: Top Shelf Aquatics – Chalice Corals Complete Guide
additional.notes: Confirmed PAR range 75–200, temperature 76–78°F, alkalinity 8–9 dKH, calcium 450 ppm, magnesium 1250–1350 ppm, feeding twice weekly. Confirmed chalice umbrella genera: Echinophyllia, Oxypora, Mycedium, Echinopora.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Echinophyllia (taxonomy and biology)
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy only: family Lobophylliidae, order Scleractinia, established by Klunzinger 1879. Synonym Oxyphyllia confirmed. Not used for husbandry parameters.
